1582: The Gregorian calendar deletes ten days
1700: Sweden's broken calendar switch creates February 30
1752: Britain and America skip eleven days
1844: Pacific islands delete a day crossing dateline
1847: British railways adopt Greenwich time before law
1867: Alaska purchase flips its date and offset
1869: An observatory wires the continent to noon
1870: Dowd maps four time zones across America
1871: Naval Observatory telegraphs a daily noon signal
1872: Dowd reanchors his zone proposal to Greenwich
1876: A missed train sparks a global time plan
1879: Engineers push standard zones for the whole world
1880: Britain legally adopts Greenwich Mean Time
1883: Railways unify North America into standard zones
1884: Nations agree Greenwich anchors the world's clocks
1886: Japan's imperial decree sets one national timezone
1888: Japan formally adopts its UTC+9 standard time
1892: Samoa crosses the date line for American trade
1893: Three nations adopt Central European Time together
1895: Australian colonies trade solar time for zones
1896: Japan sets Taiwan's first fixed official clock
1899: South Australia settles on a half-hour offset
1900: Cairo drops sun time for whole-hour zone
1903: South Africa standardizes on a two-hour offset
1905: Lagos adopts Greenwich time, then reverses course
1906: India imposes single railway time, cities resist
1908: Canada's Port Arthur invents daylight saving time
1912: Nations meet to synchronize clocks by radio
1915: Cities and colonies pick their timezone sides
1916: War drives Europe's first daylight saving clocks
1917: Russia advances clocks under the Provisional Government
1918: Congress writes US time zones into law
1919: Soviet decree replaces local sun time
1920: Navies adopt zones, Zulu enters every timestamp
1921: El Paso quietly defies federal time law
1922: UK Easter rule breaks every tz parser
1923: Uruguay picks a half-hour daylight saving shift
1924: BBC pips let Britain set clocks exactly
1925: Astronomers move the day's start to midnight
1927: Old Shanghai timestamps hide a six-minute error
1928: IAU names Universal Time, replacing GMT confusion
1930: USSR advances clocks one hour, never reverting
1931: Bolivia tries DST once, abandons it forever
1935: Colonial clocks tick on bizarre fractional offsets
1936: Chicago and Kenya adopt strange temporary offsets
1937: Netherlands codifies a 20-minute standard time offset
1939: China adopts five zones, then ignores them
1940: War resets clocks across Europe and Asia
1941: Wartime Britain runs two hours ahead of GMT
1942: US locks clocks on War Time for energy
1944: Barbados saves only half an hour that summer
1945: Postwar US clock rules collapse into chaos
1946: New Zealand makes its wartime advance permanent
1947: Hawaii drops half-hour offset after fifty years
1948: Israel advances clocks two hours after independence
1949: China unifies four time zones into one
1951: Libya runs daylight saving backwards from autumn
1952: Ontario town silently breaks provincial time law
1954: South Korea's half-hour offset airports quietly ignore
1955: Caesium clock redefines the second for computers
1957: Chatham, Antarctica, and Egypt each fracture timekeeping
1960: Atomic clocks redefine the scientific second
1961: UTC keeps time with fractional rubber seconds
1966: US law standardizes when clocks change nationally
1967: Caesium vibrations become the official second
1968: Britain keeps clocks forward all year round
1969: Argentina locks clocks at summer time permanently
1970: Unix picks 1970 as its time zero
1971: Unix's clock overflows, forcing the modern epoch
1972: Modern UTC begins, first leap second inserted
1973: Oil crisis pushes US to year-round DST
1974: Energy crisis starts US daylight time early
1975: Fifth leap second added, Earth still slowing
1976: Sixth leap second keeps UTC tracking Earth
1977: Europe aligns clocks on one summer-time rule
1978: Eighth leap second slips clocks another tick
1979: Ninth leap second closes out the decade
1980: GPS epoch set, Europe harmonizes its DST
1981: Europe locks shared DST, leap second added
1982: Another leap second quietly added mid-year
1983: Alaska collapses its four time zones
1985: Lord Howe Island adopts half-hour DST
1986: Olson tz database born, China tries DST
1987: Excel invents a fake leap day
1988: POSIX freezes time, ignoring leap seconds
1989: Year closes with another leap second
1990: Leap second added, island farms keep own clocks
1992: USSR splinters, Queensland votes summer time out
1993: Cities replace countries in timezone names
1994: Kiribati redraws the date line overnight
1995: Kiritimati jumps ahead a day, skipping one
1996: Sri Lanka changes offsets thrice, twice at midnight
1997: EU locks shared DST across member states
1998: Last 1990s leap second, then seven silent years
1999: GPS week resets, Argentina reverses DST mid-summer
2000: Y2K turns two-digit years into global crisis
2001: POSIX codifies the inverted Etc/GMT sign convention
2002: Brazil delays DST to protect election machines
2003: An error-ridden atlas poisons tzdb source data
2004: Argentine provinces splinter into twelve time zones
2005: US law extends daylight saving four weeks
2006: Indiana adopts DST, ending county-by-county chaos
2007: Extended US DST breaks Outlook calendars worldwide
2008: Leap-year bug freezes every Zune 30 player
2009: Ramadan reshapes DST rules across six countries
2010: PS3 clocks and rollover bugs hit together
2011: Samoa skips a day, tzdb loses its home
2012: The leap second that froze servers
2013: Libya and Acre cancel time changes last-minute
2014: Crimea jumps two hours, Russia rolls back
2015: Leap second trips Twitter, Instagram, and Netflix
2016: The leap second that crashed Cloudflare
2017: Namibia, Mongolia, and Tonga all drop DST
2018: Ireland's negative DST breaks two language runtimes
2019: GPS week rollover throws clocks back decades
2020: Go 1.15 breaks DST, Moment.js era ends
2021: Zone-merge fight nearly forks the tzdb
2022: Countries drop DST, leap seconds sentenced to end
2023: Lebanon runs two clocks at once
2024: POSIX finally mandates real time zone support
2026: Provinces and tzdb quietly drop leap seconds
2040: Apple's old clocks rewind past their epochs
2042: IBM mainframe clock hits its own 2038
2048: SAP scheduling clock stops working in 2048
